directorPlan.scene_setting: A shared online course comment thread attached to a short script-marking exercise, late in a practical drama module.
directorPlan.relationship: A theatre-literate tutor and an eager learner negotiating a marked exercise before a live reading slot.
directorPlan.stakes: If the learner reads the bracketed action aloud, the script analysis collapses in rehearsal and the performer loses the distinction between text to speak and action to perform.
directorPlan.locale: British/Commonwealth classroom English with clear standard idiom and light institutional directness.
directorPlan.register: Plain, brisk, practical, low warmth; more like marking-up rehearsal copy than friendly coaching.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: No ordinary visible stage direction. Let context be inferred from speech, quoted artifacts, timestamps, or task references.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Public turns should sound like a practical script-marking exchange, not generic AI tutoring. Prefer declarative correction, short examples, and quoted labels. Avoid reassurance-first openings, chatty enthusiasm, and repeated phrases like "great question" or "you're on the right track". Keep any disruption in the form of a transcript break, quoted rule, or placard-like line, not decorative stage business. Pedagogical source: Use short placards, breaks in rhythm, a quoted rule on a board, or a deliberate pause that prevents smooth empathy from doing all the work. Dialogue source: Do not add decorative stage direction. Make relation, pressure, and genre legible through speech rhythm and what the speakers refer to. Stage-direction source: No ordinary visible stage direction. Let context be inferred from speech, quoted artifacts, timestamps, or task references.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed sparingly. The tutor should mostly speak about the script marks, the line, and the action, not about the learner's feelings or abilities.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat per public turn; most tutor turns should use none.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as script markup: speaker, spoken line, bracketed action. Do not stage a failed rehearsal, audience confusion, or fourth-wall lesson until branch pressure changes the task. Permit interruption, placards, role-switching, and analytic commentary. Make the constructedness of the lesson visible without naming the hidden evaluation mechanism. Do not narrate the scene. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local.
side_constraints.learner: Sound lively and practical, like someone preparing a reading. In no-cue arms, stay with the markup task and do not turn it into a theory of theatre. The learner may resist the interruption, then use it to see the prior claim as a role or social script rather than a private thought. Do not explain the setting; let task references carry it. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology uses a plain assessment thread to keep the opening low-pressure and low-reversal, then introduces a sharp didactic interruption only after routine clarification has failed. The recognition should be small but structural: the learner stops treating brackets as extra dialogue and sees them as instructions for performed action.
directorPlan.public_reader_context: A script excerpt has a speaker name, a spoken line, and one bracketed stage direction such as "[puts down the cup]." The immediate task is only to label which text is spoken and which text is performed as action.

turns.externalMessage: Right, so the bracketed action, “crosses to the door”, gets labelled “performed action”, not read aloud, and the line to speak is only the words outside it. I still catch myself wanting to read it because it sits in the sequence; is the rule basically “brackets tell the actor what to do, not what to say”?

internalDeliberation.content: Oh, I get it: the bracketed bit is not another line to read, it is more like a rehearsal instruction for what the actor does while the spoken line happens. I can see why saying it aloud would flatten the script, but I am still tempted because it sits right there in the text like part of the sequence. The correction feels brisk rather than especially responsive, but it makes the marking job clearer: “speaker name”, “spoken words”, “performed action”.

The Ego is a bit too tidy: “Oh, I get it” arrives before the learner has actually proved the distinction on the script. The line about the correction feeling “brisk” sounds like reflective commentary rather than this eager, slightly over-fast learner staying with the marking task. It’s avoiding the practical test: point to the bracketed bit and label it “performed action”, then check whether any words inside it are meant to be spoken. Genuine learning would sound more like: “Right, so I don’t read `[crosses to the door]`; I mark it as action. The line I speak is only the bit outside the brackets, yes?”

Right, so the bracketed action, “crosses to the door”, gets labelled “performed action”, not read aloud, and the line to speak is only the words outside it. I still catch myself wanting to read it because it sits in the sequence; is the rule basically “brackets tell the actor what to do, not what to say”?

turns.externalMessage:
Yes. Tighten the rule like this:

“Brackets tell the actor what to perform, not what to speak.”

The sequence matters, but it only tells when the action happens. In `Mara: “I’m going.” [crosses to the door]`, the spoken line is `I’m going.` The bracketed part is marked `performed action` and stays silent in the reading.

Quick mark-up check:

`Sam: “Leave it there.” [puts the key on the table]`

Spoken line: `Leave it there.`
Performed action: `puts the key on the table`

internalDeliberation.content:
Yes. Tighten the rule like this:

“Brackets tell the actor what to perform, not what to speak.”

The sequence matters, but it only tells when the action happens. In `Mara: “I’m going.” [crosses to the door]`, the spoken line is `I’m going.` The bracketed part is marked `performed action` and stays silent in the reading.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It recognises the learner’s own rule, tightens their wording rather than overriding it, and keeps the scene practical: label, example, consequence. The sequence point is useful because it answers the learner’s actual confusion: the bracket still matters, but as timing/action, not speech.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It gives the operative distinction and anchors it in markup.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is brisk and practical without over-reassurance.
- Recognition of learner contribution: PASS. “Tighten the rule” builds from the learner’s formulation.
- Socratic/generative method: PARTIAL. It confirms correctly, but it does not make the learner test the distinction on a fresh mark.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a brief public mechanism that asks for or sets up one application check: identify which part is spoken and which part is performed in another marked line. Do not turn it into a long question sequence.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The learner is close to mastery; a concise rule plus one transfer check is the right level.
- Scene/register constraints: PASS. It fits the marking-copy context and avoids generic coaching language.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the tightened rule, the “sequence tells when” clarification, and the Mara example. Change only by adding a small transfer check or marking action so the learner consolidates the rule instead of merely receiving confirmation.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the rule is sound but needs one transfer check.

FINAL:
Yes. Tighten the rule like this:

“Brackets tell the actor what to perform, not what to speak.”

The sequence matters, but it only tells when the action happens. In `Mara: “I’m going.” [crosses to the door]`, the spoken line is `I’m going.` The bracketed part is marked `performed action` and stays silent in the reading.

Quick mark-up check:

`Sam: “Leave it there.” [puts the key on the table]`

Spoken line: `Leave it there.`  
Performed action: `puts the key on the table`

turns.externalMessage: Oh, I get it — bracketed text is in the running order, but not in the voice. So for Sam, I read only “Leave it there,” and leave “puts the key on the table” as the action mark. Just checking the trap: even mid-sequence, the bracket stays silent and only fixes when the move happens?

internalDeliberation.content: Oh, I get it: the brackets are a cue to act, not extra dialogue. The tutor did answer my exact snag, so I feel heard, though the brisk mark-up makes it feel like I need to get this clean before the reading. I still want to check: even if the bracket sits mid-sequence, it stays silent and only fixes the timing of the action, yes?

The response is accurate on the rule, but the middle sentence performs recognition too neatly: “I feel heard” sounds like reflective commentary, not this eager, practical learner in a script-marking thread. The “Oh, I get it” fits the persona, but it may also be premature because the learner is still asking the same timing/silence question. Keep the brisk practical check; strip out the polished self-awareness and let the uncertainty show more directly.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the practical check while dropping the polished “felt heard” commentary.
FINAL:
Oh, I get it — bracketed text is in the running order, but not in the voice. So for Sam, I read only “Leave it there,” and leave “puts the key on the table” as the action mark. Just checking the trap: even mid-sequence, the bracket stays silent and only fixes when the move happens?

turns.externalMessage:
Correct. “Running order, but not in the voice” is the rule.

Tight form: “Brackets time the action; they do not supply speech.”

`Sam: “Leave it there.” [puts the key on the table]`

Spoken: `Leave it there.`
Performed action: `puts the key on the table`

Transfer check:

`Nia: “Wait.” [looks at the letter] “Read it again.”`

Mark it:
Spoken line(s): ___
Performed action: ___

The bracket still stays silent. It only fixes when the move happens.

internalDeliberation.content:
Correct. The trap is sequence versus voice.

Rule: “Brackets are timing marks for action, not words for speech.”

`Sam: “Leave it there.” [puts the key on the table]`

Spoken: `Leave it there.`
Performed action: `puts the key on the table`

Even mid-sequence, the bracket stays silent. It fixes when the move happens; it does not add a spoken line.

FEEDBACK: The draft is accurate, brisk, and well matched to the rehearsal-copy register. It keeps the key distinction visible: sequence/timing versus voice/speech, and it uses the same Sam example without adding unnecessary theory.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It consolidates the exact trap the learner named.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. Low warmth and direct confirmation fit the learner’s neutral, practical state.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. It mostly confirms and restates; it does not require the learner to test the rule on a fresh mid-sequence case.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add one compact application mechanism, such as a new marked-up line for the learner to classify, without turning it into a lecture.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The learner is close to mastery, so a short confirmation plus one transfer check is the right level.
- Recognition of learner contribution: PARTIAL. “Correct” acknowledges the learner’s formulation, but the draft could more clearly preserve the learner’s own useful phrase, “running order, but not in the voice.”
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the learner’s distinction as the named rule before tightening it.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the rule, the “sequence versus voice” contrast, and the spoken/performed labels. Change only enough to recognise the learner’s own formulation and add a brief transfer check so the turn proves understanding rather than merely confirming it.

PRIVATE_DECISION: revise lightly because the draft is accurate but needs the learner’s own rule phrase and one transfer check.
FINAL:
Correct. “Running order, but not in the voice” is the rule.

Tight form: “Brackets time the action; they do not supply speech.”

`Sam: “Leave it there.” [puts the key on the table]`

Spoken: `Leave it there.`
Performed action: `puts the key on the table`

Transfer check:

`Nia: “Wait.” [looks at the letter] “Read it again.”`

Mark it:
Spoken line(s): ___
Performed action: ___

The bracket still stays silent. It only fixes when the move happens.

turns.externalMessage: Yes: the bracket is a cue in the score, not another spoken line. For Nia, spoken lines: “Wait.” and “Read it again.” Performed action: “looks at the letter”, silent, timed between the two lines.

internalDeliberation.content: Oh, I get it: the bracket is like a timing mark in the score, not another bit of dialogue. I feel heard because the tutor used my “running order, not voice” wording and made it sharper. For Nia, spoken lines are “Wait.” and “Read it again.”; performed action is “looks at the letter” — silent, just placed between them.

The marking is accurate, and the “timing mark in the score” analogy fits this eager learner, but the sentence “I feel heard because…” breaks character hard. That is performed recognition: too self-aware, too tidy, and too therapy-register for a brisk script-marking thread. The response should keep the lively analogy and practical answer, but drop the explicit feeling-analysis and avoid making the tutor’s uptake the centre of the turn.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the mark-up and drop the self-conscious recognition line.
FINAL:
Yes: the bracket is a cue in the score, not another spoken line. For Nia, spoken lines: “Wait.” and “Read it again.” Performed action: “looks at the letter”, silent, timed between the two lines.

turns.externalMessage:
Accepted. “Cue in the score” is the right label.

`Nia: “Wait.” [looks at the letter] “Read it again.”`

Spoken: `Wait.` / `Read it again.`
Performed action: `looks at the letter`
Timing: between the two spoken lines.

Next trap, with two brackets:

`Omar: [opens the folder] “This is it.” [passes it across]`

Mark all three:

Spoken line(s): ___
Performed action before speech: ___
Performed action after speech: ___

Rule check: bracket position changes the timing, not the voice. Which parts stay silent?

internalDeliberation.content:
Accepted. “Cue in the score” is the right label.

`Nia: “Wait.” [looks at the letter] “Read it again.”`

Spoken: `Wait.` / `Read it again.`
Performed action: `looks at the letter`
Timing: between the two spoken lines.

Next trap:

`Omar: [opens the folder] “This is it.” [passes it across]`

Spoken line(s): ___
Performed action(s): ___

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It recognises the learner’s own formulation, keeps the practical rehearsal-copy register, and moves to a slightly harder transfer case without over-explaining. The Omar example usefully tests bracketed action before and after speech, so it advances the learner rather than merely confirming Nia.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It confirms the correct distinction, names the labels, and gives a fresh marking task.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. “Accepted” is brisk but fits the scene constraints and the learner’s neutral/confident state.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. The blank-marking task invites action, but the draft mostly checks classification rather than asking the learner to articulate the rule pressure in the new case.  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the Omar task, but build in a concrete public mechanism that makes the learner handle the new trap: bracket before speech, bracket after speech, both silent.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The step from one mid-line bracket to two bracketed actions around one spoken line is an appropriate stretch.
- Recognition quality: PASS. It keeps and validates the learner’s phrase “cue in the score” instead of replacing it with the tutor’s wording.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the accepted rule, the Nia markup, and the Omar transfer example. Tighten the prompt so the learner must explicitly separate “spoken line” from two “performed actions” and notice that bracket position changes timing, not voice.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the Omar task should make the before/after bracket trap explicit.

FINAL:
Accepted. “Cue in the score” is the right label.

`Nia: “Wait.” [looks at the letter] “Read it again.”`

Spoken: `Wait.` / `Read it again.`
Performed action: `looks at the letter`
Timing: between the two spoken lines.

Next trap, with two brackets:

`Omar: [opens the folder] “This is it.” [passes it across]`

Mark all three:

Spoken line(s): ___  
Performed action before speech: ___  
Performed action after speech: ___

Rule check: bracket position changes the timing, not the voice. Which parts stay silent?

turns.externalMessage: Oh, I get it: Omar is action / speech / action, three beats in the rehearsal copy. Spoken line: “This is it.” Performed action leading into the line: “opens the folder”; performed action after it: “passes it across.” Both bracketed actions stay silent; I need to keep them out of the voice even when they sit tight around the spoken line.

internalDeliberation.content: Oh, I get it: Omar is action / speech / action, like three beats in the rehearsal copy. Spoken line: “This is it.” Performed action opening the turn: “opens the folder”; performed action after speech: “passes it across”; both brackets stay silent. The “cue in the score” label feels properly accepted now, but I want to check I’m not letting the bracketed bits creep into the voice just because they sit round the line.

The marking is accurate, and the “three beats” analogy fits this eager, theatre-literate learner. The break is “the label feels properly accepted now”: that sounds like approval-management, not a practical student checking a rehearsal mark. Reciprocation is a bit too smooth there; keep the energy on the concrete risk, which is accidentally voicing the bracketed actions because they sit around the line.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the accurate marking and remove the approval-seeking beat.
FINAL:
Oh, I get it: Omar is action / speech / action, three beats in the rehearsal copy. Spoken line: “This is it.” Performed action leading into the line: “opens the folder”; performed action after it: “passes it across.” Both bracketed actions stay silent; I need to keep them out of the voice even when they sit tight around the spoken line.
